frameworks.name: Sell to the Rich, Not From Your Own Wallet
frameworks.definition: A pricing mindset framework asserting that entrepreneurs should price for the top 10% wealth tier (net worth $1M+), not for the 50th-percentile buyer who is never their customer.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the top 10% of Americans hold a net worth of $1 million or more, meaning one in ten people has the financial capacity to buy premium offers without price resistance.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

anchors.value: limited discretionary spending; will not buy premium offers
net worth $1M+; will buy if the offer matches their perceived value

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that entrepreneurs who price based on the spending capacity of their own social circle (bottom 50% earners) are systematically underpricing, because people without money cannot advise on how to capture money from people who have it.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: entrepreneur is pricing a new offer
entrepreneur's personal network is in the bottom 50% income tier
claims.mechanism: The entrepreneur's reference group lacks both the wealth and the behavioral knowledge of wealthy buyers, so their price-sensitivity feedback is structurally wrong for that market.
bounded_by: Does not apply when the entrepreneur's peer group consists of high-net-worth individuals

claims.text: Alex Hormozi observed that a health company he worked with was so underpriced relative to its promise and delivery quality that prospects did not believe the product worked; raising the price by 50% caused close rates to increase rather than decrease.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: offer price was well below market expectation for the promise delivered
claims.mechanism: Price signals quality; a price dramatically below the implied value makes buyers doubt the offer's efficacy rather than see it as a bargain.

numbers.axis: price change magnitude vs. close rate direction

anchors.value: close rate below expected; buyers skeptical of efficacy
close rate rose (exact delta not specified)

bounded_by: Applies when price is far below the perceived value ceiling, not when price is already at or above market expectations

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends that the upsell in any offer stack should be priced at 5 to 10 times the core offer price, as long as the seller is willing to deliver that much more value.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: seller is willing to deliver 5-10x more value for 5-10x the price
claims.mechanism: A 5-10x upsell anchor reframes the core offer as a bargain by comparison, lifting core offer conversion rates even when most buyers decline the upsell.

numbers.axis: upsell price as multiple of core offer price

bounded_by: If the seller cannot deliver 5-10x more value, the fix is to reduce scope rather than lower the price

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the optimal conversion rate for a 5-10x upsell is 1 in 5 to 1 in 10, and that a seller who pitches only to buyers who can afford the upsell may find 1 in 100 accepts; expecting more acceptances signals the offer is still underpriced.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: offer is priced at the correct premium tier
audience includes a mix of wealth tiers

anchors.value: ~0 accept; mistakenly read as proof the offer is bad
potentially 1 in 10 to 1 in 100 accept (buyer just says yes without negotiation)

bounded_by: A 0% acceptance rate on a premium upsell pitched exclusively to bottom-50% prospects is not evidence the offer is flawed

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that the sweet spot for revenue is not the highest number of yes responses but the highest total dollars collected, and that maximizing yes-count and maximizing revenue are structurally opposed objectives.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: seller has the option to adjust price and accept fewer buyers
claims.mechanism: Higher-price offers collect more total profit per transaction even at far lower acceptance rates, because margin per unit dominates volume.
bounded_by: Does not apply when fixed-cost structure requires minimum volume to cover overhead

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that a business that sells only to buyers who cannot afford premium pricing will consistently underperform because those buyers are disproportionately high-friction and low-margin, and their prevalence creates a false signal that this audience represents the market.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: offer can serve both high- and low-wealth buyers
seller has not filtered for buyer wealth tier
claims.mechanism: Low-wealth buyers dominate seller attention because they are numerous, generating the false belief that difficult, low-margin deals are normal. This crowds out premium buyers who would have been easy to close.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi demonstrates via absolute profit math that selling one unit at $10,000 with a $2,000 cost produces the same net profit as selling 400 units at $50 each with a $25 cost, making low-volume high-price sales economically equivalent to high-volume low-price sales.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

numbers.axis: profit per transaction vs. required transaction volume for equivalent total profit

anchors.value: 400 units at $50 revenue, $25 cost = $10,000 revenue, $10,000 cost = same $8,000 total profit after 400x the sales effort

bounded_by: Assumes the cost structure scales linearly with volume at the low-price end

claims.text: Alex Hormozi contends that the second-worst outcome in pricing is selling a low-price item to a high-wealth buyer, because the seller forgoes the full margin the buyer was willing to pay; the cost of underpricing to a $1,000-capacity buyer on a $100 offer is $900 in missed revenue, which is disproportionately pure profit.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: The gap between a buyer's willingness to pay and the actual price is unrealized profit; this gap is largest and disproportionately high-margin at the premium buyer tier.

anchors.value: seller loses $100 (buyer cannot pay full price)
seller loses $900 in unrealized margin, most of which would have been profit

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that wealthy buyers evaluate price as a ratio to the value received rather than as an absolute number, so an offer priced at $10,000 feels expensive to a person with $100 in discretionary capital but not to a person with $1 million in net worth.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: it's only that much money to you
conditions: buyer has accumulated substantial net worth ($1M+)
claims.mechanism: Wealth converts absolute price into a price-to-wealth ratio; a $10,000 purchase represents a negligible fraction of a $1M net worth, eliminating the psychological pain that makes the same number feel large to a low-wealth buyer.
bounded_by: Does not apply when the offer's value proposition is weak regardless of price tolerance

claims.text: Alex Hormozi claims that if a seller interacts with rich buyers long enough, the seller's own wealth perception and social reference group shift toward wealth, because sustained exposure to high-wealth behavior recalibrates what feels like a normal price.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: they will make you one of them
conditions: seller consistently sells to high-net-worth buyers over an extended period
claims.mechanism: Repeated exposure to wealthy buyers normalizes their price anchors; the seller internalizes the reference class of wealthy buyers as their peer group, eliminating projection of personal price sensitivity.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that a 10x-priced upsell functions as a price anchor even when most buyers decline it: its presence makes the core offer appear to be a bargain by comparison, increasing core offer close rates and average revenue per buyer.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: upsell is presented at 10x core offer price in the same sales conversation or page
claims.mechanism: Cognitive anchoring: the buyer's reference point for what the solution costs is set by the highest price they see; the lower core offer price is evaluated against that anchor, making it feel discounted even at its original price.

examples.text: Alex Hormozi worked with a company in the health space (led by a doctor) that he believed was severely underpriced relative to the quality and research behind the offer. After sustained pushback from the owner, Hormozi secured a 50% price increase. Contrary to the owner's fear that higher prices would reduce sales, close rates went up. Hormozi attributes this to a signal-quality effect: the original price was so low compared to the promise that buyers did not believe the product could actually work.

Alex Hormozi describes the moment a first-time premium seller closes a high-ticket deal: the buyer simply says 'yeah, that sounds good' without negotiation or pushback. The seller is stunned, thinking 'I can't even believe this person would give me this much money.' Hormozi explains this is because to a high-net-worth buyer the price represents a negligible fraction of their wealth, even though the same number feels enormous to the seller who still measures it against their own financial position.

practices.text: Price your upsell at 5 to 10 times the core offer price, ensure you would be happy delivering at that level, and present it in every sales conversation regardless of how many people decline.
Stop seeking pricing feedback from friends, family, or peers in the bottom 50% income tier; they cannot advise on offers priced for buyers with $1M+ net worth.

When evaluating whether a price increase is viable, compare profit in absolute dollars (not percentages): one buyer at $10,000 with $8,000 margin eliminates the need for hundreds of low-ticket transactions.
If upsell delivery at 10x price feels like too much work, choose between raising the price further or reducing the scope of delivery; do not lower the price to match your comfort level.
